critique.summary: The article proposes using program filenames as a means of configuration, allowing software behavior to be controlled by renaming the executable file. It presents several hypothetical examples, such as reusable installers, AI experiment runners, ephemeral utilities, and P2P VPN tunnels, where the filename encodes parameters and settings to enable portability and ease of sharing.
mainPoints: Programs can read their own filenames to determine configuration and behavior.
Using filenames for configuration avoids the ephemeral nature of flags and the environment dependencies of scripts.

Examples include installers that adapt based on the filename, AI experiment runners that parse hyperparameters from the filename, utilities that perform tasks encoded in the filename, and VPN tunnels configured by filename.
This method aims to make programs self-contained, portable, and instantly shareable without additional setup or instructions.
critique.truthfulness: The concept of using filenames to influence program behavior is technically feasible and has been implemented in some niche cases, such as multi-call binaries like BusyBox. However, the article presents these ideas mostly as conceptual examples without citing real-world implementations or evidence of widespread adoption. The claims about ease of use, portability, and reproducibility are plausible but not demonstrated with empirical data or references. The article lacks citations or multiple perspectives, and the examples are hypothetical rather than proven solutions.
considerations: The approach may introduce limitations in filename length and complexity due to filesystem constraints.
Parsing complex configurations from filenames could lead to errors or reduced readability.
Security implications of encoding sensitive configuration in filenames are not discussed.
Compatibility with different operating systems and filesystems might vary.
The approach may not scale well for very complex configurations compared to traditional config files or environment variables.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the idea of using filenames as input parameters for programs instead of traditional flags or scripts. Commenters express concerns about version control, usability, and clarity, while some share nostalgic or critical perspectives on similar past approaches.

comments.author: ycombiredd
comments.text:
This just gave me a flashback to something I made a long time ago, which was a tool to create a file that was a named pipe - the contents of which were determined by the command in its filename. If I remember correctly (and its embedded man page would seem to validate this memory), the primary impetus for making this tool was to have dynamically generated files for purposes of enabling a remote process execution over server daemons that did not explicitly allow for it, such as finger, etc, but were intended only to read a specific static file.

https://web.archive.org/web/19991109163128/http://www.dfw.ne...

Using named pipes in this manner also enabled a hackish method to create dynamic server-side content by symlinking index.html to a file created in this manner, which was a secondary motivator, which seems kinda quaint and funny now, but at that time, it wasn't very long after just having finally decommed our gopher server, so fingerd was still a thing, Apache was fairly new, and I may still have been trying to convince management that the right move was not from ncsa httpd to Netscape Enteprise Server, but to Apache+mod_ssl. RSA patent licensing may still have been a thing too. Stronghold vaguely comes to mind, but I digress.

Yeah, programs that do stuff based on filename, like busybox. Oh, and this long forgotten artifact this article just reminded me of that I managed to find in the Wayback Machine, a tool to mknod a named pipe on a SunOS 4.1.4 machine, to get server-side dynamic content when remotely accessing a daemon that was supposed to return content from a single static file. Ah, memories.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed, historically grounded example that supports the article's concept, demonstrating practical use and challenges, making it highly credible and insightful.

comments.author: castral
comments.text: It feels wrong but I can't quite put my finger on the reason why... It will make version control more hectic, for sure. It also seems to be conflating identification with configuration which seems non-ideal. What about versioning and upgrading? How do I find a "well-known" entry point with a file name of flags? Every read now becomes an expensive find and grep lesson... Yeah, I don't like it.
comments.analysis: Raises valid points about maintainability and clarity issues, highlighting practical drawbacks of the approach discussed in the article.

comments.author: csb6
comments.text: Seems a lot easier to have a --help flag that lists all of the options and their function. That is self-documenting (assuming the descriptions are useful) and helps with discovery. Changing the name of the file to foo--bar.exe doesn't seem any easier than writing foo.exe --bar
comments.analysis: Offers a straightforward alternative that aligns with common CLI design principles, emphasizing usability and discoverability.

comments.author: tehbeard
comments.text: This is the classname soup mess of TailwindCSS (when people don't precompile that away) manifested into the CLI...
comments.analysis: Provides a relevant analogy that critiques the potential complexity and clutter introduced by the filename-as-input method.

comments.author: abrookewood
comments.text: You could skip the underlying mechanism by renaming Claude.exe and then it just passes the name as a new chat.
comments.analysis: Makes a brief practical suggestion related to the concept but lacks depth or broader implications.

comments.author: Quarrelsome
comments.analysis: This comment adds little substantive value and questions the seriousness of the article without elaboration.
